what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard perspective item that frames lessons from existing annotation and review tools and points to three risk areas the brief should address: anchor survival when targets change, the gap between what an agent sees and what a human saw, and mobile gesture/scroll conflicts.
observations: YAML frontmatter identifies the item as a current block sourced from claude_code in the dragnet-development channel
It links via part_of to board://item-64466290
The title prefixes the note with 'P1 · Perspective 2' while the body heading reads 'Perspective 2'
The body names Google Docs, Figma, Hypothesis, GitHub, Notion, Medium, and PDF reviewers as examples of the same comment-on-a-thing pattern
It describes the system's 'typed observation on an addressed node' as architecturally cleaner than those tools
It flags three thin areas in the brief: anchor survival, agent-vs-human view parity, and mobile gesture/scroll war
It references 'the envelope, the blocks, and the two modes' without defining them locally
standouts: Reframes the design brief through recurring failure modes of mature annotation/review products
Names three concrete, under-addressed risks: anchor survival, agent/human view mismatch, and mobile gesture/scroll conflict
Explicitly ties those risks back to the system's envelope, blocks, and two modes
conventions: Follows the ledger YAML frontmatter convention with id, address, type, source, state, author_session, channel, and links
Uses a markdown body with a heading and a bold 'My angle' framing statement
Uses a part_of link to a parent board item
Title uses a 'P1 ·' prefix that is not explained in the visible content
Written as an opinionated perspective rather than a specification or code artifact
concerns: The title contains 'P1' with no local definition
It asserts the brief is 'thinnest' in specific areas but does not show the brief or define 'envelope', 'blocks', or 'two modes'
The list of named tools is broad and unsupported by evidence inside this file
It promises 'named mechanisms and named scars' but the included excerpt is only an introductory framing paragraph
The strong evaluative language ('brutal failure modes', 'cleanliness is not safety') could be read as advocacy rather than analysis
notes: A perspective note arguing that mature comment/review tools share recurring failure modes and that the brief must harden anchor survival, agent/human view parity, and mobile interaction.
questions: What is 'the brief' and what are 'the envelope', 'the blocks', and 'the two modes'?
What does 'P1' in the title signify?
Which specific 'named mechanisms and named scars' will be detailed?
What is the exact relationship between this item and board://item-64466290?
What does 'what the agent sees vs. what Tim saw' mean concretely in this system?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to share what mature annotation/review tools learned and where they failed; the actual visible content is an introductory framing paragraph that names concerns but does not yet deliver the promised mechanisms or scars.
apparent_themes: Learn from prior art in annotation and review tools
Parity between agent-rendered and human-perceived views
Architectural cleanliness does not imply safety
Annotation as typed observations on addressed nodes
intention_signals: The note appears to be trying to import hard-won UX and architecture lessons from existing comment/review systems into the design conversation, shifting focus from elegant structure to concrete failure modes around anchoring, representation, and mobile interaction.
novelty: It treats comment-on-a-thing as a recurring machine with a shared set of scars, and it explicitly contrasts architectural cleanliness with safety, using vivid phrasing like 'gesture/scroll war on a phone'.
contribution: This file contributes a cross-product critique of annotation/review tools and flags three design risk areas—anchor survival, agent/human view parity, and mobile gesture/scroll conflict—that a fused system would keep as evaluation criteria.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard perspective item that argues mature annotation and review tools share common failure modes and warns that the brief must address anchor survival, agent/human view parity, and mobile gesture/scroll interaction.
intention_for_embedding: To bring lessons from existing comment/review products into the design evaluation of a typed-observation-on-addressed-node system.
